generations.text:
**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now, with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since you left this house, and though the days have worn on, the absence of your presence lingers like a shadow upon these walls. I have not spoken of you, nor have I written, for I feared that the words might not be enough to mend what has been broken. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself unable to remain still, and so I must try once more.

The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, once a steadfast companion, now roars with a fury that seems almost human. I have stood upon the cliffs for hours, watching the waves crash against the rocks with a violence I have never seen before. It is as if the very earth itself is weeping, and I cannot help but wonder if this is a sign—of the sea, or of my own fate.

You were always the light in this house, Eliza. When you were a child, you would come running to me with your little hands clasped around a dandelion, or a piece of driftwood, or a piece of the sea that had been caught in the tide. You had a way of seeing the beauty in the world, even when it was broken. I used to tell you that the lighthouse was not merely a place of light, but of hope.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It has been five long years since the last letter passed between us, and I find myself penning this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse. The sea is restless tonight, and the skies are black as ink. I fear I may not see dawn.

I have not written because I did not know what words could say to you. Not that I have not thought of you, day and night, in the quiet of this lonely keep. You were once my light, my only comfort in the endless dark of this life. Now, I fear I have become a shadow of myself, a man who has forgotten how to love, how to forgive, how to live.

I know you blame me for what happened. For the way I left you at the station that day, for the silence that followed, for the silence that still lingers between us. I cannot undo the past, nor can I beg your forgiveness. But I do wish to tell you this: I have never stopped loving you. Even when I could not speak it. Even when I could not be the father you needed. Even when I believed I had lost you forever.

The storm is upon us now, Eleanor. The waves are rising, crashing against the rocks with a fury that shakes the very foundation of this tower. I have lit the beacon, as I have done for thirty years, but tonight I wonder if it is for the sea,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that trembles not from the wind, but from the weight of what I must say. The sea has always been a cruel mistress, but tonight, it feels as though the very heavens are against me. The sky is bruised with clouds, the waves grow louder, and I fear the storm may claim more than just the lighthouse.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, silent years. I have not seen you, nor heard from you, and I do not know if I will ever again. You left like a shadow, disappearing into the fog, and I have been left to tend to this lonely tower, this ceaseless beacon, as though I were the only one who could keep the world from falling into darkness.

I have thought of you often, though I have never dared to write. Words, I fear, are too fragile for what I have to say. But tonight—tonight, as the tempest approaches and I feel the old bones in my body weaken—I must try.

You were once my light, my little star, and I was proud to be your father. I remember the nights you would sit by the window, wide-eyed, watching the sea, as if it were a story told in waves. You asked me so many questions, and I would tell you of the sea, of the stars, of the endless horizon. You believed in magic, in the possibility of something greater than ourselves

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink still wet from the last candle I lit, and the sea howling like a beast in the night. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than the lighthouse before dawn.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of letters unopened, of memories that cling to the walls like the salt air. I’ve tried to forget, but the sea has a way of reminding me of things I’d rather not remember.

You were once the light in my life, Eleanor. Not the light of the lighthouse, but the light that guided me through the darkest of nights. I used to tell you stories of the sea, of the stars, of the way the waves whispered secrets only the moon could understand. You listened, and I believed you would stay. But you left, and with you, a part of me died.

I’ve been alone here for so long, the loneliness has grown like the lighthouse itself—tall, proud, and a little crumbling. The other keepers have long since passed, and I’ve been left to tend to this old stone tower, this beacon of hope for those lost at sea. I’ve kept the light burning, even when the world outside seemed to have forgotten us.

But tonight is different. The wind is howling like a banshee, the waves crashing like the final toll of a funeral bell. I’ve seen storms like

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lighthouse. The sea roars, and the storm is upon us. I do not know if I shall live to see dawn, but I must say these words before the waves claim me.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I fear I have been a fool to let silence grow between us. You were but a girl when you left, and I, a man too stubborn to understand the sorrow that lay behind your departure. I did not know, then, that the sea had already taken a piece of your heart, and that you would carry its weight upon your shoulders like a burden too heavy to bear.

I have watched the horizon for many years, Eliza. Each night, I have stood at the top of this tower, the light burning bright against the dark, a beacon to those lost at sea. But tonight, I feel the light flicker—dimmed not by the storm, but by the weight of my own guilt. I have spent these years in solitude, believing that my silence was the only way to keep you safe. I was wrong. You were not safe. You were lost.

I know not what drove you away, nor do I wish to pry into the secrets you carried. But I do know this: you are not the only one who has suffered

*The lamp glows softly in the window, casting long shadows across the cold stone walls of the lighthouse. A storm brews on the horizon, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I reach for the inkwell, my hands trembling, the words forming in my mind before I can write them.*

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, not with the hope that you will read this, but with the hope that it may reach you—should I not make it through the night. Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken a word to you. I have not even written. But tonight, the wind howls with a voice I do not recognize, and I feel the weight of the sea pressing in on me like a hand on my chest.

You were ten when you left. I remember the day as if it were yesterday. You stood at the door, your face pale, your eyes filled with a sorrow I could not understand. You said you had to go, that you needed to see the world, that you could not stay here. I did not argue. I did not plead. I simply opened the door and let you go, as if I had no choice.

And you did not come back. Not once. Not for a visit, not for a letter, not even for a word. You have become a ghost in my mind, a shadow on the wall. I have tried to forget you, but

**[Sealed with a wax stamp bearing the crest of the Lighthouse of Blackmoor]**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy as the storm that brews beyond these cliffs. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, that ever-patient giant, roars with fury. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, but tonight I fear the light may not be enough to guide the lost or the damned.**

**Five years since you left, Eliza. Five years since you called me "a relic of a bygone age" and stormed out of this house as if I were a ghost. I have not spoken your name in all that time, not even to myself. Yet here I am, writing to you as if the years had not passed, as if you might still be here, still listening, still caring.**

**I know you do not believe in this place. You told me once that the sea was merely water, that the light was just a beam, that the lighthouse was nothing more than a stone tower. But you were wrong, my child. This place is more than stone and glass. It is a covenant with the heavens. It is a promise that I, a man of flesh and bone, will not let the dark take the souls of those who sail these waters. And I have kept that promise, night after night,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the devil himself is upon the cliffs, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may be my last. Yet, in this moment, I find myself compelled to speak, not only to the tempest, but to you—my child, my sorrow, my failure.

Five years have passed since the last letter you sent me, and in that time, I have not seen you, nor heard your voice. I have not spoken to you, not even in the silence of the lighthouse, where the only sound is the eternal cry of the waves. I have tried, Eleanor, I have tried to reach you, but the sea has been a cruel barrier, and your silence has been a wall I could not breach.

You were so young when you left, so full of fire and fury, and I was too proud to see the pain in your eyes. I thought I could protect you, but I only pushed you away. I remember the day you stood at the door, your face pale, your hands clenched into fists, and said, *“You don’t understand, Father.”* I did not answer. I could not. I was too afraid to say what I had always known—what I had always feared—that I had failed you.

And so you left. You walked away, as if

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not be the same as the last time I wrote. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the years have worn on me, they have not dulled the ache of your absence. I write to you now not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the quiet resolve of a man who has come to terms with the fact that some bonds are not meant to be mended, only remembered.

The sea has always been my companion, my guide, and my burden. It has been my life's work, my duty, and my sorrow. Tonight, the sea is restless, and the storm that approaches is unlike any I have seen in my years of keeping this light. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves claw at the cliffs with a fury that seems almost unnatural. I have seen many storms, my dear, but this one... this one feels different. It is as if the sea itself is weeping.

I have spent the day preparing for the worst. I have lit the lamp, though I know it may not be enough. I have secured the windows, tightened the ropes, and made sure the fire is kept alive. I have not told anyone of my fears, not even the old sailor who comes to visit from time to time. It is not that I do not trust them, but rather that I have learned to

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old lighthouse. The sea roars with a fury I have not heard since the day you left—though I dares not think of that now. Tonight, the storm is coming, and I fear it may not be the sea that takes me, but the weight of silence that has settled between us like a shroud.

Five years have passed since you last called me *father*. Five years of long nights, of watching the horizon for ships, of lighting the lamp and hoping the light might guide someone home, just as I once hoped to guide you back. But you did not come, and I did not call.

I have kept the lighthouse, as you would have wanted. I have tended the flame, as you once told me I would one day. But I have not tended to my heart, not since you left. I have let it grow cold, like the stone walls of this tower, and I have not spoken of you, not even to the sea.

I know you must think me a cruel man, a man who turned his back on his child. But I have not turned my back. I have only turned my face. I have tried to be the man you needed, the father you deserved, but I was not strong enough. I failed you, Eliza. I failed you in every way that matters.

I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart full of sorrow and longing. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with fury, as though it too knows of the storm that is to come. I have not spoken to you in five years, and now, as the night falls and the tempest brews, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, my daughter, the only light I have ever known.

I have not written to you in so long, and I fear that the words I now pen may be too late. But I must try. You were once the light in my life, the reason I kept the lighthouse burning through the darkest of nights. You were my pride, my joy, my little star that I watched from the cliffs as it twinkled in the distance. And now, you have become a shadow, a memory, a name I say to myself in the silence of the night.

I know you have been angry. I know you have blamed me for the things that have gone wrong in your life. But I ask you, my dear Eleanor, what have I done to deserve your hatred? I have given you everything—my time, my love, my very soul. And yet, you turned away. I did not know how to reach you. I did not know how to ask for forgiveness. And so, I let the years pass, and I let the silence grow.

Tonight

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast, rattling the shutters of this old lighthouse, and I find myself thinking of you more than I have in five long years. I have not spoken your name in ages, and yet it lingers in my mind like the scent of the sea on a stormy night.

You were not the only one who left. I suppose we both did, in our own ways. You vanished with your dreams, and I remained here, with the light, the salt air, and the ghosts of the past. I have kept this lighthouse for years, but I have kept my heart locked away longer.

The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than just my life. The waves grow ever louder, and the wind carries with it the sound of something ancient—something I cannot explain. I have seen things in the dark, things that make me wonder if the sea is alive, or if I have been living in a dream all along.

I do not know if I will survive the night, Eleanor. But if I do, I swear I will not let the silence between us remain. I have spent too many years alone, and I have come to understand that silence is not the same as peace. It is the echo of a love that once was, and a daughter who once called me father.

I have no

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these stone walls. The sea, which has been my constant companion for these many years, now roars with a fury I have never known. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I have not forgotten the words you spoke that day—cold, sharp, and full of pain. I have carried them like a wound, not from the words themselves, but from the silence that followed. I have tried, Eliza, I have tried to reach you, to find a way to mend what was broken. But you have turned your back, and I have not dared to follow.

I have always believed in duty, in the sacred trust of this lighthouse, the light that guides the lost and the lost at sea. But now, as I stand before the beacon that has kept countless souls from ruin, I wonder if I have been blind to the darkness within myself. The darkness of neglect, of fear, of the pain of watching you grow distant while I remained rooted in my own world.

I have no illusions, Eliza. I know you do not want to hear from me. You have every right to feel that way. I have been a father who failed to be present, a man who let the sea take more of my time than my family. I have let

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with the ink still wet and the wind howling like a wounded beast outside this old stone tower. The sea has grown restless, and the sky is bruised with clouds that hang low, as though the heavens themselves are weeping. I have spent the past hour pacing the narrow spiral stairs, my hands trembling with age and the weight of years unspoken.

Five years, Eliza. Five years since you left. Five years since the last time I heard your voice, or felt your hand in mine. I have not written, nor have I spoken. I have kept the lantern burning, the light steady, and the sea calm in its way. But inside me, it has been a storm all along.

You were never meant to leave, child. Not like that. Not with a single word and a closed door. I remember the day you came to me, your face pale, your eyes full of something I could not name. You said you needed to go, that you could not stay. I tried to stop you. I tried to plead. But you were gone before I could say more than a prayer.

I have not forgiven you, Eliza. Not yet. And perhaps I never will. But I have come to understand that you were not the only one who was lost. I was lost too, in the silence, in the shadows of this lonely tower, in the echoes of a life that once held

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the damned as the storm gathers strength beyond the cliffs. The sea, once a faithful companion, now roars with fury, as though it too has forgotten its purpose. I have stood on these shores for thirty years, guiding ships through the darkness, but tonight, I fear the light may not be enough.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five long, lonely years. I have not heard from you, nor have I written. It is not that I have no words to say—there are many—but I fear they would fall short of the weight of what I must confess. You were my light, once. The one thing that made this life bearable. And now, you are gone, like the dawn that fails to rise.

I have not spoken to you, not truly, for so long. I have told myself that it is for your own good, that you would not understand, that I would not be able to bear the sight of you, the sound of your voice. But now, as I face the storm, I wonder if I have been too afraid to let you go. If I have been too afraid to say goodbye.

You were a child once, and I was a man with a heart full of dreams. I promised you the world, and in truth, I thought I could give it to you. But the world

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health and spirits than I find myself. I write to you now, not with the ease of a man who has not yet lost his grip on the world, but with the weight of a man who knows that the night ahead may be his last.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five years of silence, of watching the sea in silence, of hearing the wind howl like a beast that knows what it is to be forgotten. I have not spoken to you, not truly, and I have not written. But tonight—tonight, the storm is coming, and I find myself with nothing but the lantern in my hands and the wind in my ears, and I think I must speak.

You were a fine girl, Eleanor. You had a fire in your soul, a light of your own. I could see it in your eyes when you were a child, when you ran up the hill to see the lighthouse, when you laughed at the sea as if it were a friend. But you grew, and I did not. I became a man who kept the light, and you became a woman who left the light behind.

I do not blame you for what you did. I do not blame you for your choices, nor for the silence that followed. I only wish I had known how to reach you, how to say the things that I could not say when you were still here.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The storm howls beyond these stone walls, a tempest that seems to mirror the storm of silence between us. I have not spoken your name in five years, and yet tonight, as the winds grow fiercer and the waves roar like the wrath of the sea, I find myself compelled to write, not to beg for your forgiveness, but to tell you the truth—before it is too late.

You were but a child when you left, a little girl with a fire in your eyes and a spirit too proud to be tamed. I tried, Eliza. I tried to be the father you needed, to guide you through the uncertainties of life. But you saw me not as a man of wisdom, but as a keeper of a lighthouse—cold, unyielding, and bound to the sea. You thought I was a stranger, when I was only afraid to be what you needed.

I have watched the years pass, and with them, the light of your presence has dimmed in my life. I have kept the lamp burning, as I always have, but the flame has grown dimmer, and the nights longer. The sea has taught me that storms do not last forever, but the silence between us has become a tempest that I fear may never pass.

You must know, my dear, that I have not forgotten you

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink still wet from the last candle I blew out, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old tower. The sea is restless, the sky a bruised shade of violet, and the waves rise like the wrath of some forgotten god. I do not know if I will see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken to you. Not a word. Not a letter. You were always the bright light in this dark, lonely place, and now you are gone, like the light that once guided the ships through these treacherous waters. I have not been the same since.

I know you think I have forgotten you. That I have turned my back on you. But I have not. Every night, I sit by the window, watching the sea, wondering if you are out there, somewhere, looking for a light that may never again guide you home.

You were only twelve when you left. I remember the day like it was yesterday. You stood at the door, your small hand gripping the doorknob, your eyes filled with tears and determination. You said you would come back, but you never did. You said you needed to find your own way, and I let you go. I thought I was doing the right thing. I thought I was protecting you.

But I was wrong.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like the wails of damned souls, the sea roiling as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The lantern still burns, though the light is dim, and the waves lash the rocks with a fury that makes me tremble. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as the storm gathers, I find myself speaking to you as if you were still here, still listening.

You were always a wild thing, my girl. I remember the day you left, how you stood at the door with your father’s coat on, your eyes like fire and your heart like stone. You said you needed freedom, that the sea was in your blood and the world was too small for you. I did not stop you, though I would have given you the world if you had stayed. I thought you were merely chasing the horizon, that you would return one day with stories and a smile. But five years have passed, and still you have not come back.

I have been alone here for so long, Eleanor. The lighthouse is my only companion, and the sea my only friend. I have watched the moon rise and fall, the stars blink out and return, and the storm clouds gather and scatter like the ghosts of your childhood. I have kept the light burning for ships that never come, and I have whispered to the wind, hoping it would carry your name to you.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the cold wind howling against the lighthouse walls, the sea roiling like a beast in torment. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than the light from the tower. I have not seen you in five years, and I do not know if I will see you again. But I must write, for I have something to say before the night is done.

You were always a fierce child, Eleanor. Too fierce, perhaps. You had the fire of a thousand suns, but it burned too bright for the world to bear. I tried to temper it, to guide it, but you would not be guided. You left, as you always have, with your head held high and your heart full of anger. I do not blame you, not entirely. I was a father who failed you, and I would rather have been a father who failed you in silence than one who spoke too much.

I have watched the sea for many years, and I have seen many storms. Some come with a whisper, others with a roar. This one... this one is different. It is not just the sea that is angry. It is the sky, the land, the very air that carries the scent of ruin. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and I have prayed that the light will guide the ships through the darkness. But tonight, I fear the light may not be enough

**April 12, 1897**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a beast outside, rattling the shutters and shaking the very bones of this old lighthouse. I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years, and I find myself writing to you now, not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the grim certainty that I may not live to see another dawn.

You were a child when you left this place, and I was a fool. I should have known that a father’s love, however fierce, could not hold a daughter who had already chosen her own path. I let pride and fear keep me from reaching out, and in doing so, I lost you. I have not forgiven myself for that. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the sea rises in fury, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years.

The light has been on for hours, burning bright through the storm, a beacon to those who might be lost at sea. It is a duty I have never neglected, and I have always believed it was my purpose here. But now, as I stand at the top of the tower, watching the sky darken with thunder and lightning, I wonder if this is my final duty. I do not fear death, Eleanor. I have lived a

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret, for I fear the storm that howls beyond these cliffs will not spare me. The sea is restless tonight, and the wind carries with it the sound of the waves, like the echoes of your laughter long gone. I have not spoken to you in five years, and now, as I sit here by the light, I find myself longing for your voice more than I ever have.

I know you would not forgive me, not after the things I said, the things I did. I was blind, Eleanor, blind to the pain I caused you. I thought I was protecting you, that I was doing what was right. But I was wrong. I was selfish, and I let my pride and fear cloud my judgment. I never once considered how much you must have suffered, how much you must have longed for my return.

The lighthouse has always been my sanctuary, my purpose, my burden. I have stood here through countless storms, weathered the tempests with only the light to guide me. But tonight, I feel the weight of something far greater than the sea. It is the weight of my silence, of my absence, of the love I could not give you when it was needed most.

I have been thinking of you, Eleanor, more than I care to admit. I see your face in the mist, in the dawn, in the flicker of the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now by the dim light of the lighthouse, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls. It is the night of the storm, and I fear it may be the last night I shall ever see. The sea has been restless all day, and the waves have grown so fierce that even the strongest of our men have taken shelter in the lower quarters. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, and I pray that it may guide the ships through this maelstrom.

I have not written to you in five years, Eleanor, and I know you have not written to me. Time has passed like the tide, and we have both been left behind by it. I do not know if you will read this letter, or if it will be lost to the sea, as so many things in our lives have been. But I must say it, for I have carried this burden too long.

You were a fine girl, Eleanor. You had a spirit like the wind, and a heart full of fire. I was proud of you, and I still am. I remember the day you left, the way you stood at the door, your eyes filled with sorrow and anger, and your voice trembling with unshed tears. You said you could not stay, that you wanted to be free. I did not understand then, and I still do not. But I know that you were right to

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is with a heart heavy as the storm that brews on the horizon that I sit to write to you. The sea howls like a wounded beast, and the wind bites with the teeth of a thousand vengeful spirits. I have not seen you in five years, and yet, as the waves crash against the cliffs and the lighthouse stands defiant against the dark, I find myself thinking of you more than ever.

You were a child when you last came to visit, and I do not know if you remember that night. It was a cold, moonless evening, and the lighthouse light had been out for hours. I had been too tired to keep it burning, and you, with your childish bravado, had insisted I turn it on again. I relented, and for a moment, the world seemed to pause. The sea, the sky, even the silence of the night—it all seemed to hold its breath as the light rose, steady and bright, cutting through the darkness like a blade.

But that was then. Now, you are gone. Or perhaps you never were. I do not know if you still remember me, or if you ever truly wanted to. You left with words like daggers, and I have carried them like a wound that never heals.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. Not a single letter, not a single word. I have kept the lighthouse, as I always

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside, and the sea, that old enemy, roaring in a fury I have not seen since the day you left. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may end with my final breath, but I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I must say what I have always wanted to say.

You were a child when you ran from me, Eliza. A child with a heart too quick to love and too sharp to forgive. I remember the day you stood at the door of our home, your eyes like shards of glass, and said, "You do not understand me, Father." And I believed you. I believed that I was not the man you needed, that I was too stern, too unyielding, too much like the sea itself—unforgiving and unchanging. And so I let you go.

But I have not forgotten. I have not forgotten the way you looked at me, the way you would sometimes come to the lighthouse at dusk, when the light was dim and the sea was calm, and you would stand at the edge of the cliff, as if searching for something you could not name. I would call to you, and you would turn, and for a moment, the world would still, and I would see in your eyes the same light that burns in the beacon of this

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, with the wind howling like a wounded beast and the sea roaring as though it would swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. The sky is black as pitch, and the waves—ah, the waves—they come with a fury that makes my bones tremble. I have stood here, in this lighthouse, for over thirty years, and never before have I felt so alone. But tonight, I think I may not survive it. And if I do not, I would have you know that I have not forgotten you.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have not softened the ache of your absence. I have seen your name in the ledger, once or twice, when the wind was still and the sea was calm, and I would have liked to call out to you, to ask you to come back. But I did not. I did not dare. And now, I wonder if I ever will.

You were a proud girl, Eleanor. Too proud, perhaps. You had your own dreams, your own path, and I—well, I was content in this place, in this duty, in this quiet life. I thought it was enough. I thought I could keep you safe from the world, even as it kept me from you. But the world is not kind, and neither am I.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

The lighthouse stands strong, though I am not so certain of myself. Five years have passed since you left this house, and I have not heard a word from you. I have not seen your face, nor heard your voice, nor felt the warmth of your presence. You were once my greatest joy, and now you are a shadow in my mind, a ghost in the wind. I have tried to forget, but the sea does not forget. It carries my thoughts, my regrets, my sorrow, out to the horizon where you once stood.

I know you must think me a cruel man, a man who abandoned you. But I did not abandon you, Eleanor. I tried to protect you.
